Coloured engraving by H. D. Smith, commemorating the grant of a charter in 1829 to [King's College, London](/wiki/King%27s_College,_London "King's College, London")

A **royal charter** is a formal grant issued by a monarch under [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative") as [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent"). Historically, they have been used to promulgate [public laws](/wiki/Public_law "Public law"), the most famous example being the English [Magna Carta](/wiki/Magna_Carta "Magna Carta") (great charter) of 1215, but since the 14th century have only been used in place of [private acts](/wiki/Private_act "Private act") to grant a right or power to an individual or a [body corporate](/wiki/Body_corporate "Body corporate").[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) They were, and are still, used to establish significant organisations such as boroughs (with [municipal charters](/wiki/Municipal_charter "Municipal charter")), [universities](/wiki/University "University") and [learned societies](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society"), and were historically used to establish [companies](/wiki/Chartered_company "Chartered company").

Charters should be distinguished from [royal warrants of appointment](/wiki/Royal_warrant_of_appointment "Royal warrant of appointment"), [grants of arms](/wiki/Grant_of_arms "Grant of arms"), and other forms of letters patent, such as those granting an organisation the right to use the word "royal" in their name or granting [city status](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom"), which do not have legislative effect.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-PC-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The British monarchy [has issued over 1,000 royal charters](/wiki/List_of_organisations_in_the_United_Kingdom_with_a_royal_charter "List of organisations in the United Kingdom with a royal charter").[[5]](#cite_note-PC-5) Of these about 750 remain in effect.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The earliest charter recorded on the UK Privy Council's list was granted to the [Worshipful Company of Weavers](/wiki/Worshipful_Company_of_Weavers "Worshipful Company of Weavers") by [Henry I of England](/wiki/Henry_I_of_England "Henry I of England") on 17 November 1155,[[5]](#cite_note-PC-5) although older charters are known to have existed, including [Malcolm III](/wiki/Malcolm_III_of_Scotland "Malcolm III of Scotland")'s charter to the town of [Tain](/wiki/Tain "Tain") in Scotland in 1066[[8]](#cite_note-8) and [William I](/wiki/William_I_of_England "William I of England")'s charter to the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") in 1067.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Charters continue to be issued by the British [Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"), a recent example being that awarded to the [Chartered Institute of Legal Executives](/wiki/Chartered_Institute_of_Legal_Executives "Chartered Institute of Legal Executives") (CILEX), and [the Chartered Institute of Ergonomics and Human Factors](/wiki/The_Chartered_Institute_of_Ergonomics_and_Human_Factors "The Chartered Institute of Ergonomics and Human Factors"), in 2014.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Historical development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Historical development")]

Charters have been used in [Europe since medieval times](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") to grant rights and privileges to towns, boroughs, and cities. During the 14th and 15th centuries the concept of incorporation of a municipality by royal charter evolved.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Royal charters were used in England to make the most formal grants of various rights, titles, etc. until the reign of [Henry VIII](/wiki/Henry_VIII "Henry VIII"), with [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent") being used for less solemn grants. After the eighth year of Henry VIII, all grants under the [Great Seal](/wiki/Great_Seal "Great Seal") were issued as letters patent.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Among the past and present groups formed by royal charter are the [Company of Merchants of the Staple of England](/wiki/Merchants_of_the_Staple "Merchants of the Staple") (13th century), the [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company") (1600), the [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company"), the [Chartered Bank of India, Australia & China](/wiki/Chartered_Bank_of_India,_Australia_%26_China "Chartered Bank of India, Australia & China") (since merged into [Standard Chartered](/wiki/Standard_Chartered "Standard Chartered")), [P&O](/wiki/P%26O "P&O"), the [British South Africa Company](/wiki/British_South_Africa_Company "British South Africa Company"), and some of the [former British colonies on the North American mainland](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas"), [City livery companies](/wiki/Livery_companies "Livery companies"), the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") and the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") (see [BBC Charter](/wiki/BBC_Charter "BBC Charter")).[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Corporations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Corporations")]

Between the 14th and 19th centuries, royal charters were used to create [chartered companies](/wiki/Chartered_company "Chartered company") – for-profit ventures with shareholders, used for exploration, trade, and colonisation. Early charters to such companies often granted trade monopolies, but this power was restricted to Parliament from the end of the 17th century.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Until the 19th century, royal charters were the only means other than an [act of parliament](/wiki/Act_of_parliament "Act of parliament") by which a company could be [incorporated](/wiki/Incorporation_(business) "Incorporation (business)"); in the UK, the [Joint Stock Companies Act 1844](/wiki/Joint_Stock_Companies_Act_1844 "Joint Stock Companies Act 1844") opened up a route to incorporation by registration, since when incorporation by royal charter has been, according to the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council_of_the_United_Kingdom "Privy Council of the United Kingdom"), "a special token of Royal favour or ... a mark of distinction".[[5]](#cite_note-PC-5)[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The use of royal charters to incorporate organisations gave rise to the concept of the "corporation by prescription". This enabled corporations that had existed from [time immemorial](/wiki/Time_immemorial "Time immemorial") to be recognised as incorporated via the [legal fiction](/wiki/Legal_fiction "Legal fiction") of a "lost charter".[[17]](#cite_note-17) Examples of corporations by prescription include [Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford") and [Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") universities.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Universities and colleges

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Universities and colleges")]

See also: [University charter](/wiki/University_charter "University charter")

According to the *[Catholic Encyclopedia](/wiki/Catholic_Encyclopedia "Catholic Encyclopedia")*, of the 81 universities established in pre-Reformation Europe, 13 were established *ex consuetudine* without any form of charter, 33 by [Papal bull](/wiki/Papal_bull "Papal bull") alone, 20 by both Papal bull and [imperial](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor") or royal charter, and 15 by imperial or royal charter alone. Universities established solely by royal (as distinct from imperial) charter did not have the same international recognition – their degrees were only valid within that kingdom.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

The first university to be founded by charter was the [University of Naples](/wiki/University_of_Naples "University of Naples") in 1224, founded by an imperial charter of [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Frederick II, Holy Roman Emperor"). The first university founded by royal charter was the [University of Coimbra](/wiki/University_of_Coimbra "University of Coimbra") in 1290, by [King Denis of Portugal](/wiki/King_Denis_of_Portugal "King Denis of Portugal"), which received papal confirmation the same year. Other early universities founded by royal charter include the [University of Perpignan](/wiki/University_of_Perpignan "University of Perpignan") (1349; papal confirmation 1379) and the University of [Huesca](/wiki/Huesca "Huesca") (1354; no confirmation), both by [Peter IV of Aragon](/wiki/Peter_IV_of_Aragon "Peter IV of Aragon"); the [Jagiellonian University](/wiki/Jagiellonian_University "Jagiellonian University") (1364; papal confirmation the same year) by [Casimir III of Poland](/wiki/Casimir_III_of_Poland "Casimir III of Poland"); the [University of Vienna](/wiki/University_of_Vienna "University of Vienna") (1365; Papal confirmation the same year) by [Rudolf IV, Duke of Austria](/wiki/Rudolf_IV,_Duke_of_Austria "Rudolf IV, Duke of Austria"); the [University of Caen](/wiki/University_of_Caen "University of Caen") (1432; Papal confirmation 1437) by [Henry VI of England](/wiki/Henry_VI_of_England "Henry VI of England"); the [University of Girona](/wiki/University_of_Girona "University of Girona") (1446; no confirmation) and the [University of Barcelona](/wiki/University_of_Barcelona "University of Barcelona") (1450; papal confirmation the same year), both by [Alfonso V of Aragon](/wiki/Alfonso_V_of_Aragon "Alfonso V of Aragon"); the [University of Valence](/wiki/University_of_Valence "University of Valence") (1452; papal confirmation 1459) by the [Dauphin](/wiki/Dauphin_of_France "Dauphin of France") Louis (later [Louis XI of France](/wiki/Louis_XI_of_France "Louis XI of France")); and the [University of Palma](/wiki/University_of_the_Balearic_Islands "University of the Balearic Islands") (1483; no confirmation) by [Ferdinand II of Aragon](/wiki/Ferdinand_II_of_Aragon "Ferdinand II of Aragon").[[21]](#cite_note-Kivinen-21)

#### British Isles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: British Isles")]

Both Oxford and Cambridge received royal charters during the 13th century. However, these charters were not concerned with academic matters or their status as universities but rather about the exclusive right of the universities to teach, the powers of the [chancellors'](/wiki/Chancellor_(university) "Chancellor (university)") courts to rule on disputes involving students, and fixing rents and interest rates.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The University of Cambridge was confirmed by a papal bull in 1317 or 1318,[[24]](#cite_note-24) but despite repeated attempts, the University of Oxford never received such confirmation.[[21]](#cite_note-Kivinen-21) The three [pre-Reformation Scottish universities](/wiki/Ancient_universities_of_Scotland "Ancient universities of Scotland") were all established by papal bulls: [St Andrews](/wiki/University_of_St_Andrews "University of St Andrews") in 1413; [Glasgow](/wiki/University_of_Glasgow "University of Glasgow") in 1451; and [King's College, Aberdeen](/wiki/King%27s_College,_Aberdeen "King's College, Aberdeen") (which later became the [University of Aberdeen](/wiki/University_of_Aberdeen "University of Aberdeen")) in 1494.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Following the Reformation, establishment of universities and colleges by royal charter became the norm. The [University of Edinburgh](/wiki/University_of_Edinburgh "University of Edinburgh") was founded under the authority of a royal charter granted to the [Edinburgh town council](/wiki/The_City_of_Edinburgh_Council "The City of Edinburgh Council") in 1582 by [James VI](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") as the "town's college". [Trinity College Dublin](/wiki/Trinity_College_Dublin "Trinity College Dublin") was established by a royal charter of [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") (as [Queen of Ireland](/wiki/Queen_of_Ireland "Queen of Ireland")) in 1593. Both of these charters were given in [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The Edinburgh charter gave permission for the town council "to build and to repair sufficient houses and places for the reception, habitation and teaching of professors of the schools of grammar, the humanities and languages, philosophy, theology, medicine and law, or whichever liberal arts which we declare detract in no way from the aforesaid mortification" and granted them the right to appoint and remove professors.[[27]](#cite_note-27) But, as concluded by Edinburgh's principal, [Sir Alexander Grant](/wiki/Sir_Alexander_Grant,_10th_Baronet "Sir Alexander Grant, 10th Baronet"), in his tercentenary history of the university, "Obviously this is no charter founding a university".[[28]](#cite_note-28) Instead, he proposed, citing multiple pieces of evidence, that the surviving charter was original granted alongside a second charter founding the college, which was subsequently lost (possibly deliberately).[[29]](#cite_note-29) This would also explain the source of Edinburgh's degree awarding powers, which were used from the foundation of the college.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The royal charter of Trinity College Dublin, while being straightforward in incorporating the college, also named it as "mother of a University", and rather than granting the college degree-awarding powers stated that "the students on this College ... shall have liberty and power to obtain degrees of Bachelor, Master, and Doctor, at a suitable time, in all arts and faculties".[[31]](#cite_note-31) Thus the [University of Dublin](/wiki/University_of_Dublin "University of Dublin") was also brought into existence by this charter, as the body that awards the degrees earned by students at Trinity College.[[32]](#cite_note-TCD_Legal_FAQ-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Following this, no surviving universities were created in the British Isles until the 19th century. The 1820s saw two colleges receive royal charters: [St David's College, Lampeter](/wiki/St_David%27s_College,_Lampeter "St David's College, Lampeter") in 1828 and [King's College London](/wiki/King%27s_College_London "King's College London") in 1829. Neither of these were granted degree-awarding powers or university status in their original charters. The 1830s saw an attempt by [University College London](/wiki/University_College_London "University College London") to gain a charter as a university and the creation by Act of Parliament of [Durham University](/wiki/Durham_University "Durham University"), but without incorporating it or granting any specific powers. These led to debate about the powers of royal charters and what was implicit to a university.

The essence of the debate was firstly whether the power to award degrees was incidental to the creation of a university or needed to be explicitly granted and secondly whether a royal charter could, if the power to award degrees was incidental, limit that power – UCL wishing to be granted a royal charter as "London University" but excluding the power to award degrees in theology due to the secular nature of the institute. Sir [Charles Wetherell](/wiki/Charles_Wetherell "Charles Wetherell"), arguing against the grant of a royal charter to UCL before the Privy Council in 1835, argued for degree-awarding powers being an essential part of a university that could not be limited by charter.[[34]](#cite_note-34) [Sir William Hamilton](/wiki/Sir_William_Hamilton,_9th_Baronet "Sir William Hamilton, 9th Baronet"), wrote a response to Wetherell in the [Edinburgh Review](/wiki/Edinburgh_Review "Edinburgh Review"), drawing in Durham University and arguing that the power of universities, including the power to award specific degrees, had always been explicitly granted historically, thus creating a university did not implicitly grant degree-awarding powers.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Other historians, however, disagree with Hamilton on the point of whether implicit grants of privileges were made, particularly with regard to the *ius ubique docendi* – the important privilege of granting universally-recognised degrees that was the defining mark of the *[studium generale](/wiki/Studium_generale "Studium generale")*. [Hastings Rashdall](/wiki/Hastings_Rashdall "Hastings Rashdall") states that "the special privilege of the *jus ubique docendi* ... was usually, but not quite invariably, conferred in express terms by the original foundation-bulls; and was apparently understood to be involved in the mere act of erection even in the rare cases where it is not expressly conceded".[[36]](#cite_note-36) Similarly, Patrick Zutshi, Keeper of Manuscripts and University Archives in Cambridge University Library, writes that "Cambridge never received from the papacy an explicit grant of the *ius ubique docendi*, but it is generally considered that the right is implied in the terms of John XXII's letter of 1318 concerning Cambridge's status as a studium generale."[[37]](#cite_note-37)

UCL was incorporated by royal charter in 1836, but without university status or degree-awarding powers, which went instead to the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London"), created by royal charter with the explicit power to grant degrees in Arts, Law, and Medicine. Durham University was incorporated by royal charter in 1837 (explicitly not founding the university, which it describes as having been "established under our Royal sanction, and the authority of our Parliament") but although this confirmed that it had "all the property, rights, and privileges which ... are incident to a University established by our Royal Charter" it contained no explicit grant of degree-awarding powers.[[38]](#cite_note-38) This was considered sufficient for it to award "degrees in all the faculties",[[39]](#cite_note-39) but all future university royal charters explicitly stated that they were creating a university and explicitly granted degree-awarding power. Both London (1878) and Durham (1895) later received supplemental charters allowing the granting of degrees to women, which was considered to require explicit authorisation. After going through four charters and a number of supplemental charters, London was reconstituted by Act of Parliament in 1898.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The Queen's Colleges in Ireland, at [Belfast](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_Belfast "Queen's University Belfast"), [Cork](/wiki/University_College_Cork "University College Cork"), and [Galway](/wiki/NUI_Galway "NUI Galway"), were established by royal charter in 1845, as colleges without degree awarding powers. The [Queen's University of Ireland](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_of_Ireland "Queen's University of Ireland") received its royal charter in 1850, stating "We do will, order, constitute, ordain and found an University ... and the same shall possess and exercise the full powers of granting all such Degrees as are granted by other Universities or Colleges in the faculties of Arts, Medicine and Law".[[41]](#cite_note-41) This served as the degree awarding body for the Queen's Colleges until it was replaced by the [Royal University of Ireland](/wiki/Royal_University_of_Ireland "Royal University of Ireland").

The royal charter of the [Victoria University](/wiki/Victoria_University_(United_Kingdom) "Victoria University (United Kingdom)") in 1880 started explicitly that "There shall be and is hereby constituted and founded a University" and granted an explicit power of awarding degrees (except in medicine, added by supplemental charter in 1883).[[42]](#cite_note-42)

From then until 1992, all universities in the United Kingdom were created by royal charter except for [Newcastle University](/wiki/Newcastle_University "Newcastle University"), which was separated from Durham via an Act of Parliament. Following the independence of the [Republic of Ireland](/wiki/Republic_of_Ireland "Republic of Ireland"), new universities there have been created by Acts of the [Oireachtas](/wiki/Oireachtas "Oireachtas") (Irish Parliament). Since 1992, most new universities in the UK have been created by [Orders of Council](/wiki/Order_of_Council "Order of Council") as secondary legislation under the [Further and Higher Education Act 1992](/wiki/Further_and_Higher_Education_Act_1992 "Further and Higher Education Act 1992"), although granting degree-awarding powers and university status to colleges incorporated by royal charter is done via an amendment to their charter or a supplemental charter. Following the [Higher Education and Research Act 2017](/wiki/Higher_Education_and_Research_Act_2017 "Higher Education and Research Act 2017"), degree awarding powers in England are normally conferred by [statutory instruments](/wiki/Statutory_instrument "Statutory instrument") made by the [Office for Students](/wiki/Office_for_Students "Office for Students"). The Office for Students also has authority to vary or revoke degree-awarding powers and university titles granted by royal charter by requiring institutions to amend their charter, with the act having given the secretary of state the power to make the necessary changes to their charter if they fail to do so (although not to revoke the charter in its entirety).[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: United States")]

Several of the [colonial colleges](/wiki/Colonial_colleges "Colonial colleges") that predate the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") are described as having been established by royal charter. Except for [The College of William & Mary](/wiki/The_College_of_William_%26_Mary "The College of William & Mary"), which received its charter from [King William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") and [Queen Mary II](/wiki/Queen_Mary_II "Queen Mary II") in 1693 following a mission to London by college representatives, these were either provincial charters granted by local governors (acting in the name of the king) or charters granted by legislative acts from local assemblies.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The first charters to be issued by a colonial governor on the consent of their council (rather than by an act of legislation) were those granted to [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") (as the College of New Jersey) in 1746 (from acting governor [John Hamilton](/wiki/John_Hamilton_(New_Jersey_politician) "John Hamilton (New Jersey politician)")) and 1748 (from Governor [Jonathan Belcher](/wiki/Jonathan_Belcher "Jonathan Belcher")). There was concern as to whether a royal charter given by a governor in the King's name was valid without royal approval. An attempt to resolve this in London in 1754 ended inconclusively when [Henry Pelham](/wiki/Henry_Pelham "Henry Pelham"), the prime minister, died. However, Princeton's charter was never challenged in court prior to its ratification by the state legislature in 1780, following the US Declaration of Independence.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

[Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") received its royal charter (as King's College) in 1754 from Lieutenant Governor [James DeLancey](/wiki/James_DeLancey "James DeLancey") of New York, who bypassed the assembly rather than risking it rejecting the charter.[[47]](#cite_note-47) [Rutgers University](/wiki/Rutgers_University "Rutgers University") received its (as Queen's College) in 1766 (and a second charter in 1770) from Governor [William Franklin](/wiki/William_Franklin "William Franklin") of New Jersey,[[48]](#cite_note-48) and [Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College") received its in 1769 from Governor [John Wentworth](/wiki/Sir_John_Wentworth,_1st_Baronet "Sir John Wentworth, 1st Baronet") of New Hampshire.[[49]](#cite_note-49) The case of *[Dartmouth College v. Woodward](/wiki/Dartmouth_College_v._Woodward "Dartmouth College v. Woodward")*, heard before the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") in 1818, centred on the status of the college's royal charter.[[50]](#cite_note-50) The court found in 1819 that the charter was a contract under the [Contract Clause](/wiki/Contract_Clause "Contract Clause") of the US Constitution, meaning that it could not be impaired by state legislation, and that it had not been dissolved by the revolution.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

The charter for the College of William and Mary specified it to be a "place of universal study, or perpetual college, for divinity, philosophy, languages and other good arts and sciences", but made no mention of the right to award degrees.[[52]](#cite_note-52) However, the Latin text of the charter uses *[studium generale](/wiki/Studium_generale "Studium generale")* – the technical term used in the Middle Ages for a university –where the English text has "place of universal study"; it has been argued that this granted William and Mary the rights and status of a university.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

The Princeton charter, however, specified that the college could "give and grant any such degree and degrees ... as are usually granted in either of our universities or any other college in our realm of Great Britain".[[54]](#cite_note-54) Columbia's charter used very similar language a few years later,[[55]](#cite_note-55) as did Dartmouth's charter.[[56]](#cite_note-56) The charter of Rutger uses quite different words, specifying that it may "confer all such honorary degrees as usually are granted and conferred in any of our colleges in any of our colonies in America".[[57]](#cite_note-57)

Of the other colleges founded prior to the American Revolution, [Harvard College](/wiki/Harvard_College "Harvard College") was established in 1636 by Act of the Great and General Court of the Massachusetts Bay Colony and incorporated in 1650 by a charter from the same body,[[58]](#cite_note-58) [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") was established in 1701 by Act of the General Assembly of Connecticut,[[59]](#cite_note-59) the [University of Pennsylvania](/wiki/University_of_Pennsylvania "University of Pennsylvania") received a charter from the proprietors of the colony in 1753,[[60]](#cite_note-60) [Brown University](/wiki/Brown_University "Brown University") was established in 1764 (as the College of Rhode Island) by an Act of the Governor and General Assembly of Rhode Island,[[61]](#cite_note-61) and [Hampden-Sydney College](/wiki/Hampden-Sydney_College "Hampden-Sydney College") was established privately in 1775 but not incorporated until 1783.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Canada")]

Eight Canadian universities and colleges were founded or reconstituted under royal charters in the 19th century, prior to [Confederation](/wiki/Canadian_Confederation "Canadian Confederation") in 1867. Most Canadian universities originally established by royal charter were subsequently reincorporated by acts of the relevant parliaments.[[63]](#cite_note-CanadaUni-63)

The [University of King's College](/wiki/University_of_King%27s_College "University of King's College") was founded in 1789 and received a royal charter in 1802, naming it, like Trinity College, Dublin, "the Mother of an University" and granting it the power to award degrees.[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) The charter remains in force.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Arts_Building%2C_McGill_University%2C_Aug_31_2022.jpg/250px-Arts_Building%2C_McGill_University%2C_Aug_31_2022.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arts_Building,_McGill_University,_Aug_31_2022.jpg)

The McGill University [Arts Building](/wiki/Arts_Building_(McGill_University) "Arts Building (McGill University)") in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec")

[McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") was established under the name of *McGill College* in 1821, by a provincial royal charter issued by Governor General of British North America [the Earl of Dalhousie](/wiki/George_Ramsay,_9th_Earl_of_Dalhousie "George Ramsay, 9th Earl of Dalhousie"); the charter stating that the "College shall be deemed and taken to be an University" and should have the power to grant degrees.[[67]](#cite_note-67) It was reconstituted by a royal charter issued in 1852 by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Queen_Victoria "Queen Victoria"), which remains in force.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

The [University of New Brunswick](/wiki/University_of_New_Brunswick "University of New Brunswick") was founded in 1785 as the Academy of Liberal Arts and Sciences and received a provincial charter as the College of New Brunswick in 1800. In the 1820s, it began giving university-level instruction and received a royal charter under the name *King's College* as a "College, with the style and privileges of an University", in 1827. The college was reconstituted as the University of New Brunswick by an act of the provincial parliament in 1859.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)

The [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto") was founded by royal charter in 1827, under the name of *King's College*, as a "College, with the style and privileges of an University", but did not open until 1843. The charter was subsequently revoked and the institution replaced by the University of Toronto in 1849, under provincial legislation.[[71]](#cite_note-71) [Victoria University](/wiki/Victoria_University,_Toronto "Victoria University, Toronto"), a college of the University of Toronto, opened in 1832 under the name of the *Upper Canada Academy*, giving "pre-university" classes, and received a royal charter in 1836. In 1841. a provincial act replaced the charter, reconstituted the academy as Victoria College, and granted it degree-awarding powers.[[72]](#cite_note-72) Another college of the University of Toronto, [Trinity College](/wiki/Trinity_College,_Toronto "Trinity College, Toronto"), was incorporated by an act of the legislature in 1851 and received a royal charter in 1852, stating that it, "shall be a University and shall have and enjoy all such and the like privileges as are enjoyed by our Universities of our United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland".[[73]](#cite_note-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Staufferlibrary.JPG/250px-Staufferlibrary.JPG)](/wiki/File:Staufferlibrary.JPG)

[Stauffer Library](/wiki/Stauffer_Library "Stauffer Library") at [Queen's University](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_at_Kingston "Queen's University at Kingston") in [Kingston, Ontario](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario")

[Queen's University](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_at_Kingston "Queen's University at Kingston") was established by royal charter in 1841. This remains in force as the university's primary constitutional document and was last amended, through the Canadian federal parliament, in 2011.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

[Université Laval](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_Laval "Université Laval") was founded by royal charter in 1852, which granted it degree awarding powers and started that it would, "have, possess, and enjoy all such and the like privileges as are enjoyed by our Universities of our United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland".[[75]](#cite_note-75) This was replaced by a new charter from the [National Assembly of Quebec](/wiki/National_Assembly_of_Quebec "National Assembly of Quebec") in 1971.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

[Bishop's University](/wiki/Bishop%27s_University "Bishop's University") was founded, as Bishop's College, by an act of the Parliament of the Province of Canada in 1843 and received a royal charter in 1853, granting it the power to award degrees and stating that, "said College shall be deemed and taken to be a University, and shall have and enjoy all such and the like privileges as are enjoyed by our Universities of our United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland".[[77]](#cite_note-77)

The [University of Ottawa](/wiki/University_of_Ottawa "University of Ottawa") was established in 1848 as the College of Bytown. It received a royal charter under the name *College of Ottawa*, raising it to university status in 1866.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

#### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Australia")]

The older Australian universities of [Sydney](/wiki/University_of_Sydney "University of Sydney") (1850) and [Melbourne](/wiki/University_of_Melbourne "University of Melbourne") (1853) were founded by acts of the legislatures of the colonies. This gave rise to doubts about whether their degrees would be recognised outside of those colonies, leading to them seeking royal charters from London, which would grant legitimacy across the British Empire.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

The [University of Sydney](/wiki/University_of_Sydney "University of Sydney") obtained a royal charter in 1858. This stated that (emphasis in the original):

> the Memorialists confidently hope that the Graduates of the University of Sydney will not be inferior in scholastic requirements to the majority of Graduates of British Universities, and that it is desirable to have the degrees of the University of Sydney generally recognised throughout our dominions; and it is also humbly submitted that although our Royal Assent to the Act of Legislature of New South Wales hereinbefore recited fully satisfies the principle of our law that the power of granting degrees should flow from the Crown, yet that as that assent was conveyed through an Act which has effect only in the territory of New South Wales, the *Memorialists believe that the degrees granted by the said University under the authority of the said Act, are not legally entitled to recognition beyond the limits of New South Wales*; and the Memorialists are in consequence most desirous to obtain a grant from us of Letters Patent requiring all our subjects to recognise the degrees given under the Act of the Local Legislature in the same manner as if the said University of Sydney had been an University established within the United Kingdom under a Royal Charter or an Imperial enactment.

The charter went on to (emphasis in the original):

> will, grant and declare that the Degrees of Bachelor of Arts, Master of Arts, Bachelor of Laws, Doctor of Laws, Bachelor of Medicine, and Doctor of Medicine, already granted or conferred or hereafter to be granted or conferred by the Senate of the said University of Sydney shall be recognised as Academic distinctions and rewards of merit *and be entitled to rank, precedence, and consideration in* our United Kingdom and in our Colonies and possessions throughout the world *as fully as if the said Degree had been granted by any University of our said United Kingdom*.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

The University of Melbourne's charter, issued the following year, similarly granted its degrees equivalence with those from British universities.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

The act that established the [University of Adelaide](/wiki/University_of_Adelaide "University of Adelaide") in 1874 included women undergraduates, causing a delay in the granting of its charter as the authorities in London did not wish to allow this. A further petition for the power to award degrees to women was rejected in 1878 – the same year that London was granted that authority. A charter was finally granted – admitting women to degrees – in 1881.[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)

The last of Australia's 19th century universities, the [University of Tasmania](/wiki/University_of_Tasmania "University of Tasmania"), was established in 1890 and obtained a royal charter in 1915.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### Guilds, learned societies, and professional bodies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Guilds, learned societies, and professional bodies")]

Guilds and livery companies are among the earliest organisations recorded as receiving royal charters. The Privy Council list has the [Saddlers Company](/wiki/Saddlers_Company "Saddlers Company") in 1272 as the earliest, followed by the [Merchant Taylors Company](/wiki/Merchant_Taylors_Company "Merchant Taylors Company") in 1326 and the [Skinners Company](/wiki/Skinners_Company "Skinners Company") in 1327. The earliest charter to the Saddlers Company gave them authority over the saddlers trade; it was not until 1395 that they received a charter of incorporation.[[85]](#cite_note-85) The Merchant Taylors were similarly incorporated by a subsequent charter in 1408.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

Royal charters gave the first regulation of medicine in Great Britain and Ireland. The [Barbers Company](/wiki/Barbers_Company "Barbers Company") of London in 1462, received the earliest recorded charters concerning medicine or surgery, charging them with the superintendence, scrutiny, correction, and governance of surgery. A further charter in 1540 to the London Guild – renamed the Company of Barber-Surgeons – specified separate classes of surgeons, barber-surgeons, and barbers. The London Company of Surgeons separated from the barbers in 1745, eventually leading to the establishment of the [Royal College of Surgeons](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Surgeons "Royal College of Surgeons") by royal charter in 1800.[[87]](#cite_note-Ross-87) The [Royal College of Physicians of London](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Physicians_of_London "Royal College of Physicians of London") was established by royal charter in 1518 and charged with regulating the practice of medicine in the City of London and within seven miles of the City.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

The [Barbers Guild](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Surgeons_in_Ireland "Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland") (the *Gild of St Mary Magdalen*) in Dublin is said to have received a charter in 1446, although this was not recorded in the rolls of chancery and was lost in the 18th century. A later charter united the barbers with the (previously unincorporated) surgeons in 1577.[[89]](#cite_note-89) The [Royal College of Physicians of Ireland](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Physicians_of_Ireland "Royal College of Physicians of Ireland") was established by royal charter in 1667[[90]](#cite_note-90) and the [Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland](/wiki/Royal_College_of_Surgeons_in_Ireland "Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland"), which evolved from the Barbers' Guild in Dublin, in 1784.[[91]](#cite_note-91)

The [Royal Society](/wiki/Royal_Society "Royal Society") was established in 1660 as Britain's first [learned society](/wiki/Learned_society "Learned society") and received its first royal charter in 1662. It was reincorporated by a second royal charter in 1663, which was then amended by a third royal charter in 1669. These were all in Latin, but a supplemental charter in 2012 gave an English translation to take precedence over the Latin text.[[92]](#cite_note-92) The [Royal Society of Edinburgh](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Edinburgh "Royal Society of Edinburgh") was established by royal charter in 1783 and the [Royal Irish Academy](/wiki/Royal_Irish_Academy "Royal Irish Academy") was established in 1785 and received its royal charter in 1786.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

New professional bodies were formed in Britain in the early 19th century representing new professions that arose after the industrial revolution and the rise of *[laissez-faire](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire")* capitalism. These new bodies sought recognition by gaining royal charters, laying out their constitutions and defining the profession in question, often based on occupational activity or particular expertise. To their various corporate objectives, these bodies added the concept of working in the public interest that was not found in earlier professional bodies. This established a pattern for British professional bodies, and the 'public interest' has become a key test for a body seeking a royal charter.[[94]](#cite_note-94)

## Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Australia")]

Royal charters have been used in Australia to incorporate non-profit organisations. However, since at least 2004 this has not been a recommended mechanism.[[95]](#cite_note-95)

## Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Belgium")]

The royal decree is the equivalent in [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium") of a royal charter. In the period before 1958, 32 higher education institutes had been created by royal charter. These were typically engineering or technical institutions rather than universities.[[96]](#cite_note-96)

However, several non-technical higher education institutions have been founded, or refounded, under royal decree, such as the *[Fonds de la Recherche Scientifique](/wiki/Fonds_de_la_Recherche_Scientifique "Fonds de la Recherche Scientifique")* (National Fund for Scientific Research) in 1928[[97]](#cite_note-97) and the *[Koninklijke Vlaamse Academie van België voor Wetenschappen en Kunsten](/wiki/Koninklijke_Vlaamse_Academie_van_Belgi%C3%AB_voor_Wetenschappen_en_Kunsten "Koninklijke Vlaamse Academie van België voor Wetenschappen en Kunsten")* in 1938.[[98]](#cite_note-98)

Since [the Belgian state reform of 1988–1989](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium#1988-1989:_The_third_state_reform "State reform in Belgium"), competency over education was transferred to the federated entities of Belgium. Royal decrees can therefore no longer grant higher education institution status or university status.[[99]](#cite_note-99)

## Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Hudsonsbayco.jpg/250px-Hudsonsbayco.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hudsonsbayco.jpg)

The [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company") building in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal")

In Canada, there are a number of organisations that have received royal charters. However, the term is often applied incorrectly to organisations, such as the [Royal Astronomical Society of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Astronomical_Society_of_Canada "Royal Astronomical Society of Canada"), that have been granted the use of a royal title rather than a royal charter.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

### Companies and societies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Companies and societies")]

Companies, corporations, and societies in Canada founded under or augmented by a royal charter include:

* The [Canada Company](/wiki/Canada_Company "Canada Company"), incorporated by Act of Parliament in June 1825. A royal charter was issued in August 1826 to purchase and develop lands. Purchased the Crown Reserve of 1,384,413 acres and a special grant of 1,100,000 acres in the Huron County area.[[101]](#cite_note-books.google.ca-101)
* The [Literary and Historical Society of Quebec](/wiki/Literary_and_Historical_Society_of_Quebec "Literary and Historical Society of Quebec"), founded in 1824 as the first learned society in Canada, received its royal charter in 1831.[[102]](#cite_note-102)
* The [Royal Society of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Canada "Royal Society of Canada"), founded by Act of Parliament and granted a royal charter in 1883.[[103]](#cite_note-103)
* The [Royal Life Saving Society of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Life_Saving_Society_of_Canada "Royal Life Saving Society of Canada"), founded 1891 and received royal patronage and style 1904. A royal charter was granted in 1924 by King George V.[[104]](#cite_note-104)

British royal chartered corporations operating in Canada:

* The [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"); granted a royal charter in 1600 by Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I") (tea sales in North America)[[101]](#cite_note-books.google.ca-101)
* The [Hudson's Bay Company](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_Company "Hudson's Bay Company"); founded by a royal charter issued in 1670 by [King Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") (administration of parts of current Quebec, Northern Ontario, and North West Territories, including Manitoba, Saskatchewan, and Alberta, and judicial connections with Upper Canada)[[105]](#cite_note-Hudson's_Bay_Company-105)
* The [Bank of British North America](/wiki/Bank_of_British_North_America "Bank of British North America") capital raised in Britain, founded by royal charter issued in 1836 (amalgamated with [Bank of Montreal](/wiki/Bank_of_Montreal "Bank of Montreal") 1918).[[105]](#cite_note-Hudson's_Bay_Company-105)
* The [Royal Commonwealth Society](/wiki/Royal_Commonwealth_Society "Royal Commonwealth Society"); founded by a royal charter issued in 1882 by [Queen Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_of_the_United_Kingdom "Victoria of the United Kingdom")[[106]](#cite_note-106)
* The [Royal Academy of Dance](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Dance "Royal Academy of Dance"); founded in 1920 as the Association of Teachers of Operatic Dancing; reconstituted by a royal charter issued in 1936 by [King George V](/wiki/King_George_V "King George V")[[107]](#cite_note-107)
* [The Boy Scouts Association](/wiki/The_Scout_Association "The Scout Association") founded in 1910; incorporated by royal charter in 1912; Canadian General Council, now called [Scouts Canada](/wiki/Scouts_Canada "Scouts Canada"), formed in 1914 and incorporated by Act of the Canadian Parliament in 1914.

### Territories and communities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Territories and communities")]

Cities under royal charter are not subject to municipal Acts of Parliament applied generally to other municipalities, and instead are governed by legislation applicable to each city individually. The royal charter codifies the laws applied to the particular city and lays out the powers and responsibilities not given to other municipalities in the province concerned.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [St. John's](/wiki/St._John%27s,_Newfoundland_and_Labrador "St. John's, Newfoundland and Labrador"); claimed as England's first oversea colony by royal charter issued in 1583 by Queen [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I")
* [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"); founded by a royal charter issued in 1621 by King [James I](/wiki/James_I_of_England "James I of England")[[108]](#cite_note-108)
* [Saint John](/wiki/Saint_John,_New_Brunswick "Saint John, New Brunswick"); founded by a royal charter issued in 1785 by King [George III](/wiki/George_III_of_the_United_Kingdom "George III of the United Kingdom")[[109]](#cite_note-109)
* [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver")
* [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg")
* [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal")[[110]](#cite_note-110)

## India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: India")]

The [Institution of Engineers](/wiki/Institution_of_Engineers_(India) "Institution of Engineers (India)") was incorporated by royal charter in 1935.[[111]](#cite_note-111)

## Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Ireland")]

A number of Irish institutions were established by or received royal charters prior to Irish independence. These are no longer under the jurisdiction of the British Privy Council and their charters can thus only be altered by a Charter or Act of the [Oireachtas](/wiki/Oireachtas "Oireachtas") (Irish Parliament).[[32]](#cite_note-TCD_Legal_FAQ-32)

## South Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: South Africa")]

The [University of South Africa](/wiki/University_of_South_Africa "University of South Africa") received a royal charter in 1877.[[112]](#cite_note-112) The [Royal Society of South Africa](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_South_Africa "Royal Society of South Africa") received a royal charter in 1908.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

## United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

Main article: [List of organisations in the United Kingdom with a royal charter](/wiki/List_of_organisations_in_the_United_Kingdom_with_a_royal_charter "List of organisations in the United Kingdom with a royal charter")

See also: [List of professional associations in the United Kingdom § Chartered](/wiki/List_of_professional_associations_in_the_United_Kingdom#Chartered "List of professional associations in the United Kingdom")

Royal charters continue to be used in the United Kingdom to incorporate charities and [professional bodies](/wiki/British_professional_bodies "British professional bodies"), to raise [districts](/wiki/Non-metropolitan_district "Non-metropolitan district") to [borough status](/wiki/Borough_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "Borough status in the United Kingdom"), and to grant university status and degree awarding powers to colleges previously incorporated by royal charter.[[114]](#cite_note-Commons-114)

Most new grants of royal charters are reserved for professional institutions or charities that "work in the public interest" and "can demonstrate pre-eminence, stability and permanence in their particular field".[[115]](#cite_note-privy-115) The body in question has to demonstrate not just pre-eminence and financial stability but also that bringing it under public regulation in this manner is in the public interest.[[116]](#cite_note-116) The decision to grant or refuse a royal charter can be subject to [judicial review](/wiki/Judicial_review "Judicial review"). This occurred for the first time in 2016, when the decision to grant a royal charter to the (British) [Association for Project Management](/wiki/Association_for_Project_Management "Association for Project Management") (APM) was unsuccessfully challenged in the court by the (American) [Project Management Institute](/wiki/Project_Management_Institute "Project Management Institute") (PMI).[[114]](#cite_note-Commons-114) PMI feared the grant of a charter would give a competitive advantage to APM and claimed the criteria had not been correctly applied. The courts ruled that while the possibility of suffering a competitive disadvantage did give PMI standing to challenge the decision, the Privy Council was permitted to take the public interest (in having a chartered body promoting the profession of project management) into account as outweighing any failure to meet the criteria in full.[[117]](#cite_note-117)

Individual [chartered designations](/wiki/Chartered_(professional) "Chartered (professional)"), such as [chartered accountant](/wiki/Chartered_accountant "Chartered accountant") or [chartered engineer](/wiki/Chartered_engineer "Chartered engineer"), are granted by some chartered professional bodies to individual members that meet certain criteria. The Privy Council's policy is that all chartered designations should be broadly similar, and most require master's level qualifications (or similar experience).[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-Charter_FAQ-119)

In January 2007, the UK Trade Marks Registry refused to grant protection to the American [Chartered Financial Analyst](/wiki/Chartered_Financial_Analyst "Chartered Financial Analyst") trademark, as the word "chartered" in the UK is associated with royal charters, thus its use would be misleading.[[120]](#cite_note-120) "Charter" and "chartered" continue to be "sensitive words" in company names, requiring evidence of a royal charter or (for "chartered") permission from a professional body operating under royal charter.[[121]](#cite_note-121) The use of "chartered" in a [collective trade mark](/wiki/Collective_trade_mark "Collective trade mark") similarly requires the association applying for the mark to have a royal charter as otherwise "the mark would mislead the public into believing that the association and its members have chartered status".[[122]](#cite_note-122)

Unlike other royal charters, a charter to raise a district to borough status is issued using statutory powers under the [Local Government Act 1972](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1972 "Local Government Act 1972") rather than by the royal prerogative.[[119]](#cite_note-Charter_FAQ-119)

The [company registration number](/wiki/Companies_House "Companies House") of a corporation with a royal charter is prefixed by "RC" for companies registered in England and Wales, "SR" for companies registered in Scotland, and "NR" for companies registered in Northern Ireland.[[123]](#cite_note-123) However, many chartered corporations from outside England and Wales have an RC prefix from when this was used universally, including chartered universities such as the University of Dundee[[124]](#cite_note-124) and the University of Ulster.[[125]](#cite_note-125)

The [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") operates under a royal charter which, unusually, is reviewed and renewed every ten years.[[114]](#cite_note-Commons-114)[[126]](#cite_note-126) In 2026, the British government announced that they intended to grant the BBC a permanent charter in order to protect it from political interference.[[127]](#cite_note-127)

## United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Royal_charter&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: United States")]

Royal charters have not been issued in the US since independence. Those that existed prior to that have the same force as other charters of incorporation issued by state or colonial legislatures. Following *[Dartmouth College v. Woodward](/wiki/Dartmouth_College_v._Woodward "Dartmouth College v. Woodward")*, they are "in the nature of a contract between the state, the corporation representing the founder, and the objects of the charity". Case law indicates that they cannot be changed by legislative action in a way that impairs the original intent of the founder, even if the corporation consents.[[128]](#cite_note-128)

34. **[^](#cite_ref-34)** Charles Wetherell (1834). [*Substance of the Speech of Sir Charles Wetherell: Before the Lords of the Privy Council, on the Subject of Incorporating the London University*](https://archive.org/details/substancespeech01wethgoog). J. G. & F. Rivington. pp. [77](https://archive.org/details/substancespeech01wethgoog/page/n87)–82. "It will be necessary to examine this subject a little more minutely, and particularly with reference to the power of conferring degrees, and the nature of a university. The only place where I can find any legal discussion on matters so little brought under consideration as these, is the argument of Mr. Attorney General Yorke, in Dr. Bentley's case, which is reported in 2nd Lord Raymond, 1345 ... In this proposition of Mr. Yorke two principles are laid down. The first is that 'granting degrees flows from the Crown;' and the second is, that if 'a University be erected, the power of granting degrees is incidental to the grant.' ... The subject matter granted, is the power of covering degrees; an emanation, as Mr. Yorke expresses it, from the Crown. It is the concession of this power that constitutes the direct purpose and the essential character of a University. ... This question of law arises:– How can this anomalous and strange body be constituted in the manner professed? It is to be a 'University,' but degrees in theology it is not to give. But Mr. Attorney-General Yorke tells us, that the power of giving degrees is incidental to the grant. If this be law, is not the power of conferring theological degrees equally incident to the grant, as other degrees; and if this be so, how can you constitute a University without the power of giving 'all' degrees: The general rule of law undoubtedly is, that where a subject matter is granted which has legal incidents belonging to it, the incidents must follow the subject granted; and this is the general rule as to corporations; and it has been decided upon that principle, that as a corporation, as an incident to its corporate character, has a right to dispose of its property, a proviso against alienation is void."

"[p. 492] But when it has been seriously argued before the Privy Council by Sir Charles Wetherell, on behalf of the English Universities ... that the simple fact of the crown incorporating an academy under the name of university, necessarily, and in spite of reservations, concedes to that academy the right of granting all possibly degrees; nay when (as we are informed) the case itself has actually occurred, – the 'Durham University,' inadvertently, it seems, incorporated under that title, being in the course of claiming the exercise of this very privilege as a right, necessarily involved in the public recognition of the name: – in these circumstances we shall be pardoned a short excursus, in order to expose the futility of the basis on which this mighty edifice is erected. [p. 497] ... in all the Universities throughout Europe, which were not merely privileged, but created by bull and charter, every liberty conferred was conferred not as an *incident* through implication, but by express conversion. And this in two ways:– For a university was empowered, either by an explicit grant of certain enumerated rights, or by bestowing on it implicitly the known privileges enjoyed by other pattern Universities"
